title: Node Class and Linked List Basics

description.task: Implement a Node class and a BasicLinkedList class. The list should support append (add to end), prepend (add to front), and print operations. Demonstrate creation of a linked list and traversal.
instructions: Node constructor: set this.data=data and this.next=null
append(data): if head is null, set head=new Node(data); else traverse to last node (next==null) and set next=new Node(data)
prepend(data): create new Node(data), set newNode.next=head, set head=newNode
print() and size() are already implemented — do not modify them
After append(1,2,3) and prepend(0): list is 0->1->2->3, size=4
description.hint: append: Node curr=head; while(curr.next!=null) curr=curr.next; curr.next=new Node(data). prepend: Node n=new Node(data); n.next=head; head=n;

title: Linked List vs Array Memory Allocation

description.task: Implement a MemoryComparison class that demonstrates the key difference between array (contiguous allocation) and linked list (scattered node allocation) by simulating addressOf for nodes. Each node has a data field and a pointer field.
instructions: showLinkedListLayout(n): simulate n nodes with addresses starting at 2000, incrementing by 28 each
For each node i (0-based): print 'node[i] @ address A, data=i+1, next-ptr -> B' where B is the address of the next node
For the last node, print 'next-ptr -> NULL' instead of an address
showArrayLayout is already implemented as a reference
Output must show the scattered vs contiguous contrast
description.hint: int base=2000, step=28; for(int i=0;i<n;i++){ int addr=base+i*step; String next=(i<n-1)?String.valueOf(base+(i+1)*step):"NULL"; System.out.printf('  node[%d] @ address %d, data=%d, next-ptr -> %s%n',i,addr,i+1,next); }
description.expectedOutput:
Array layout (contiguous):
  arr[0] @ address 1000 = 1
  arr[1] @ address 1004 = 2
  arr[2] @ address 1008 = 3
  arr[3] @ address 1012 = 4
Linked list layout (scattered):
  node[0] @ address 2000, data=1, next-ptr -> 2028
  node[1] @ address 2028, data=2, next-ptr -> 2056
  node[2] @ address 2056, data=3, next-ptr -> 2084
  node[3] @ address 2084, data=4, next-ptr -> NULL

description.task: Implement an InsertionComparison class that counts shift operations needed to insert into the middle of an array vs the pointer updates needed to insert into the middle of a linked list. Compare the two approaches for increasing n.
instructions: arrayMiddleInsertShifts(n): return n - n/2 (integer division; for n=10 returns 5)
linkedListMiddleInsertOps(n): return n/2 + 2 (traverse to middle + 2 pointer assignments)
compare() is already implemented — only the two calculation methods need code
For n=10: array=5, LL=7; for n=100: array=50, LL=52; for n=1000: array=500, LL=502
comment on why LL eventually wins for pure insertion (once traversal is O(1))
description.hint: arrayMiddleInsertShifts: return n - n/2; linkedListMiddleInsertOps: return n/2 + 2;
description.expectedOutput:
n=10 | Array shifts: 5 | LL pointer ops: 7
n=100 | Array shifts: 50 | LL pointer ops: 52
n=1000 | Array shifts: 500 | LL pointer ops: 502
